evidence.quote: The installation of the butterfly valve was intended to be a temporary change, and the system drawings had not been updated to reflect the new valve/actuator type, the addition of a pressure regulator and pressure gage, and the change from
There was no recognition at the time that this change would allow the full 350 psig system pressure to bypass the regulator gage, and there would be no visible pressure indication to the operator.
Relative perceptions of low-pressure systems versus high-pressure systems, utilizing inert commodities, and complacency can lead to design errors, and operational mistakes.

impact_notes: Hardware ejection with potential for serious personnel injury; no actual injuries sustained but personnel were in immediate vicinity of over-pressurized actuator failure.

title: Innovate Safe Spacecraft States Prior To Impending Failures During Operations
abstract_text: Proactive development of off-nominal and failure spacecraft scenarios allows considered evaluation of options and team buy-in before a crisis occurs.
driving_event_text: Failure of the second of four reaction wheels during Kepler mission.
lesson_text: Throughout the K2 mission, potential adverse spacecraft scenarios, which were not considered during the original mission development, were envisioned. These included safing due to fuel exhaustion, safing without reaction wheels, data rates below 100 bps, and no DSN contacts for weeks at a time. In each case the scenario was discovered and mitigated well before it was necessary, allowing plenty of time to test on the ground and, in some cases, in flight.
recommendation_text: Do not wait until situations are dire to look for remedies. Continually look ahead to what issues might be coming in the future and start mitigating as soon as possible. This allows for more time not just to test, but to get buy-in from all necessary parties before it becomes a crisis situation.

evidence.quote: potential adverse spacecraft scenarios, which were not considered during the original mission development, were envisioned
Do not wait until situations are dire to look for remedies. Continually look ahead to what issues might be coming
allowing plenty of time to test on the ground and, in some cases, in flight

impact_notes: Second reaction wheel failure during Kepler/K2 mission required development of multiple off-nominal safing scenarios post-launch; proactive scenario planning prevented operational crisis.

title: Tailor Risk Posture And Anomaly Resolution Process In Response To Criticality Of On-Orbit Events
abstract_text: As knowledge of the flight system increases with experience, mission data collection efficiency can be increased prudently by reducing the amount of anomaly oversight and streamlining the decision-making.
driving_event_text: The Kepler mission was launched in April of 2009 into an Earth-trailing orbit with the goal of searching for Earth-sized planets in the habitable zone around sun-like stars. The Kepler mission completed its primary mission in November of 2012 and was approved for an extended mission two years beyond that, with the option of two more if the spacecraft remained healthy. Lessons were learned over an extended period of time during Kepler/K2 Mission Ops. The date shown is roughly at the Kepler/K2 transition.
lesson_text: Early in the Kepler mission the operations team had little experience with both the spacecraft and the in-flight risks asso ciated with the mission. As such, anomaly responses involved large teams of engineers and even larger committees that were required to approve any small step toward recovery. Later in the mission the spacecraft and relative risks were much better understood, and the oversight required to make effective decisions dropped dramatically. An example is the safing of the spacecraft due to a processor power-on reset. Once the underlying cause was established and the response was standardized, an Anomaly Resolution Board was no longer necessary to approve of recovery in subsequent events. This streamlined process significantly improved the efficiency of science data collection later in the mission.
recommendation_text: Adjust the anomaly process to better suit the evolving risk posture of the mission and the improved experience of the team. As the mission goes on the committee becomes less and less useful at contributing to a more positive outcome. More dramatic or novel anomalies can be addressed by bringing in more oversight or expertise as needed, but the level of oversight and bureaucracy that is prudent early on can interfere with timely and effective responses later in the mission.

evidence.quote: Early in the Kepler mission the operations team had little experience with both the spacecraft and the in-flight risks associated with the mission. As such, anomaly responses involved large teams of engineers and even larger committees that

Once the underlying cause was established and the response was standardized, an Anomaly Resolution Board was no longer necessary to approve of recovery in subsequent events. This streamlined process significantly improved the efficiency of
the level of oversight and bureaucracy that is prudent early on can interfere with timely and effective responses later in the mission

impact_notes: Improved science data collection efficiency through streamlined anomaly resolution process; no safety or mission-critical impact stated.

title: Experiences with Remote Work as Part of the Kepler/K2 Project at NASA Ames Research Center
abstract_text: The purpose of this paper is to highlight the benefits, risks, and mitigation strategies of remote work at NASA Ames. This analysis is based upon my experience of working for the Kepler/K2 project (kepler.nasa.gov), which utilized a space-based telescope for over 9 years to detect planets around other stars and study a wide variety of astrophysical phenomena. The mission interfaced with a multitude of partners both inside and outside of NASA to oversee spacecraft construction and operations, community management, and provide data products, documentation, and scientific papers for the Astronomical community. I have worked for the Kepler/K2 project for 7 years --- 1 year on-site followed by 6 years remote, with the first 4.5 years as a Support Scientist and the last 2.5 years as the Science Office Director. As a result, this discussion is most pertinent to projects looking to retain personnel, but some of it likely also applicable to new team formation. There are four main areas relating to remote work that I address in this paper: Team Composition and Performance, IT Access and Technical Support, Timezone Differentials, and Costs. For each category I discuss the benefits of remote work, the associated risks, the mitigation techniques to reduce or eliminate those risks, and finally a quotreality checkquot where I discuss how many of the identified issues are often encountered by co-located teams, and thus not entirely unique to remote work.
driving_event_text: See attached PDF.
lesson_text: See attached PDF.
recommendation_text: See attached PDF.

Team Composition and Performance, IT Access and Technical Support, Timezone Differentials, and Costs...risks, the mitigation techniques

title: High Oxidizer Vapor Content May Cause Vapor Reaction in Bi-Propellant Systems
abstract_text: Propulsion subsystem check valves on the Juno spacecraft malfunctioned during preparations for a bi-propellant main engine orbital maneuver. Although the failure mechanism had no major impact on the Juno mission, it poses a risk that an engine may operate outside of its qualified mixture ratio, which could lead to mission loss. If full life testing is not feasible, the propulsion subsystem should ensure positive isolation of the propellant vapors throughout the mission. Also, assure sufficient downlink telemetry to ascertain the health and functionality of the propulsion subsystem.
driving_event_text: The successful 35-minute Jupiter Orbit Insertion (JOI) maneuver on July 4, 2016 allowed the planet Jupiter to capture the Juno spacecraft. The NASA/Caltech Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) then began preparing for the final major burn designed to change its orbit, with a period of 54 days, to its 14-day nominal operational science orbit. The final major burn to slow Juno and place the spacecraft in a tighter orbit around the planet (Figure 1) was planned as a period reduction maneuver (PRM), sometimes referred to for this mission as a Perijove Reduction Maneuver (although geometric perijove was not being changed by the maneuver). The PRM was intended as a regulated main engine burn in which both the fuel and oxidizer tank pressures are regulated during the burn by a helium pressurization system. This regulatory system is connected to the fuel and oxidizer tanks via passive check valves, and pyrolitic isolation valves in the oxidizer side (Figure 2).​ Figure 1. Juno’s orbital grid During preparations for the Juno PRM on October 13, 2016, telemetry on tank pressure readings revealed an anomaly during the pre-PRM tank pressurization activity (Reference (1)). A series of check valves between the helium tank and the fuel tanks remained closed for about four minutes after imposition of a positive pressure difference that should have immediately opened them. Further review of telemetry showed the oxidizer check valves had also malfunctioned in the same mode but to a lesser extent during the first few minutes of the prior burn-- the JOI maneuver performed in July. The telemetry further showed unexpected gas-side pressure increases suggestive of unintended upstream flow when the oxidizer tank pressure exceeded the gas-side pressure during the Deep Space Maneuver (DSM-2) performed 45 months earlier. Figure 2. A simplified Juno Propulsion Subsystem block diagram. Juno utilizes a weight-saving and redundant approach to spacecraft propulsion, with a bi-propellant main propulsion system and a monopropellant Reaction Control System. The main propulsion system uses hydrazine as fuel and nitrogen tetroxide as oxidizer. Lower than expected pressures in either the fuel or oxidizer tanks can result in unintended oxidizer-to-fuel mixture ratios in the main engine during the burn, with potential effects ranging from under-performance (to nominal performance) to catastrophic failure. Hence, the “stuck-closed check valve” anomaly eventually led to a decision by the Juno project to forego the PRM altogether. The most likely cause for the pre-PRM fuel check valve anomaly was contamination, with the contaminant consisting of vapor fuel-oxidizer reaction products (FORP). The pre-JOI failure of the oxidizer check valve to immediately open is believed to be caused by excessive oxidizer exposure; but it could also be FORP contamination, with either possibility caused by an oxidizer leak failure that occurred in the previous maneuver (DSM-2). According to this scenario, the earlier oxidizer check valve leak failure (at DSM-2) produced excessive amounts of oxidizer in the gas side leading to: The oxidizer check valves sticking closed (at pre-JOI). The high oxidizer vapor content creating FORP at the fuel check valves. The FORP leading to the subsequent “stuck closed check valve” anomaly at pre-PRM. Because FORP contamination was a well–known failure mode during Juno design and development, multiple safeguards, such as quad-redundant check valves and oxidizer-side pyrolitic isolation valves, were employed. But these measures were obviously insufficient. While an anomaly resolution team determined that the fuel tank could be pressurized and a main engine burn executed in a blowdown mode to execute the PRM-- even in the presence of the anomalous check valves-- the project elected to avoid the risk of possibly attaining a less-than-desirable orbit and remain in the 53-day capture orbit for the remainder of the Juno mission. The higher-than-planned orbital period does not affect the quality of the science collected by Juno during each flyby because the altitude over Jupiter is the same at the time of closest approach. In terms of the total quantity of science return, it has recently been announced (Reference (3)) that the Juno mission will be extended three years until at least July 2021 so it can complete the additional 18 perijoves needed to finish mapping Jupiter. Another outcome of the longer orbit is that Juno will spend less time within Jupiter’s radiation belts on each orbit, reducing the accumulated radiation exposure of the spacecraft. References: “PRM Pressurization Check Valve Issue,” JPL Incident Surprise Anomaly (ISA) No. 60324, JPL-internal document, October 13, 2016. “Juno Check Valve Anomaly Recovery Discussion,” JPL-internal document, Juno Project, December 15, 2016. https://prs.jpl.nasa.gov/NET/DownloadAttachment.aspx?iAttachmentID=60144 (JPL internal access only). “NASA Re-plans Juno's Jupiter Mission,” Jet Propulsion Laboratory, June 6, 2018. https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/news.php?feature=7153 .
lesson_text: In bi-propellant systems there is a risk that high oxidizer vapor content may create vapor FORP at the fuel check valve, resulting in a sticking propulsion check valve during an in-flight maneuver and anomalous engine operation, which could lead to loss of mission. The current method used by Juno and other spaceflight missions to limit propulsion system contact with propellant vapor to benign concentrations relies largely on quad-redundant check valves, and pyrolitic isolation valves in the oxidizer side. The Juno anomaly exemplifies the risk that high oxidizer vapor content may cause a sticking check valve during a maneuver that will result in the engine operating well outside of the qualified mixture ratio; this may lead to loss of the mission. However, the Juno mission is on track to achieve its mission objectives due largely to the availability of sufficient downlinked propulsion system health data to accurately characterize the system state.
recommendation_text: Note that there are multiple candidate design measures for ensuring propellant vapors never meet, including: A propulsion subsystem design that uses separate pressurant tanks, similar to the Europa Clipper design. Use of check valves that prevent vapor back-flow. For example, such check calves were used successfully by the Cassini project, which employed a combination of latch valves and pyro valves like Juno, but the Cassini valves were no longer available for acquisition by the Juno project. Incorporate pyro valves in both propellant sides and reduce the time the tanks are not isolated. In designing spacecraft propulsion subsystems for long-duration missions: Adhere to JPL’s test-as-you-fly (TAYF) principle when flight-qualifying check valves. (That is, life testing would likely have detected the Juno oxidizer leak that led to either FORP build-up or excessive exposure of the check valve to oxidizer.) This recommendation may be very difficult to implement because it requires full mission-duration, high-fidelity testing lasting years. If TAYF is not feasible for a project, the propulsion subsystem should ensure positive isolation of the propellant vapors throughout the mission. This is in contrast to the current practice of seeking to limit the propellant vapor contact to benign concentrations, partly by means of pyrolitic isolation valves. Projects should continue to incorporate sufficient downlink telemetry to ascertain the health and functionality of the propulsion subsystem.

evidence.quote: life testing would likely have detected the Juno oxidizer leak that led to either FORP build-up or excessive exposure of the check valve to oxidizer
high oxidizer vapor content may create vapor FORP at the fuel check valve, resulting in a sticking propulsion check valve during an in-flight maneuver
Juno mission is on track to achieve its mission objectives due largely to the availability of sufficient downlinked propulsion system health data to accurately characterize the system state

impact_notes: Risk of mission loss if engine operates outside qualified mixture ratio during maneuver. Juno avoided loss due to redundant check valves and telemetry; however, anomaly delayed PRM by ~20 months and required mission replanning.

title: That's How We Roll: The NASA K2 Mission Science Products and Their Performance Metrics
nasa_topics: Engineering design and project processes and standards
abstract_text: NASA's exoplanet Discovery mission Kepler was reconstituted as the K2 mission a year after the failure of the second of Kepler's four reaction wheels in 2013 May. Fine control of the spacecraft pointing is now accomplished through the use of the two remaining well-functioning reaction wheels and balancing the pressure of sunlight on the solar panels, which constrains K2 observations to fields in the ecliptic for up to approximately 80 days each. This pseudo-stable mechanism gives typical roll motion in the focal plane of 1.0 pixels peak-to-peak over 6 hr at the edge of the field, two orders of magnitude greater than typical 6 hr pointing errors in the Kepler primary mission. Despite these roll errors, the joint performance of the flight system and its modified science data processing pipeline restores much of the photometric precision of the primary mission while viewing a wide variety of targets. The improvements in K2 operations and science data analysis resulting from 1.5 years of experience with this new mission concept, and quantified by the metrics in this paper, will support continuation of K2's already high level of scientific productivity in an extended K2 mission.
driving_event_text: NASA's exoplanet Discovery mission Kepler was reconstituted as the K2 mission a year after the failure of the second of Kepler's four reaction wheels in 2013 May. Fine control of the spacecraft pointing is now accomplished through the use of the two remaining well-functioning reaction wheels and balancing the pressure of sunlight on the solar panels, which constrains K2 observations to fields in the ecliptic for up to approximately 80 days each.
lesson_text: The improvements in K2 operations and science data analysis resulting from 1.5 years of experience with this new mission concept, and quantified by the metrics in this paper, will support continuation of K2's already high level of scientific productivity in an extended K2 mission.
recommendation_text: We define K2 performance metrics for data compression and pixel budget available in each campaign; the photometric noise on exoplanet transit and stellar activity timescales; residual correlations in corrected long-cadence light curves; and the protection of test sinusoidal signals from overfitting in the systematic error removal process. We find that data compression and noise both increase linearly with radial distance from the center of the field of view, with the data compression proportional to star count as well. At the center, where roll motion is nearly negligible, the limited 6 hr photometric precision for a quiet 12th magnitude star cam be as low as 30 ppm, only 25% higher than that of Kepler. This noise performance is achieved without sacrificing signal fidelity; test sinusoids injected into the data are attenuated by less than 10% for signals with periods up to 15 days, so that a wide range of stellar rotation and variability signatures are preserved by the K2 pipeline.

evidence.quote: failure of the second of Kepler's four reaction wheels in 2013 May
roll motion in the focal plane of 1.0 pixels peak-to-peak over 6 hr at the edge of the field, two orders of magnitude greater than typical 6 hr pointing errors
improvements in K2 operations and science data analysis resulting from 1.5 years of experience with this new mission concept

impact_notes: Loss of reaction wheel redundancy forced mission reconstitution with degraded pointing control (100× worse roll errors). Recovery required 1.5 years of operational learning and pipeline modifications to restore near-primary-mission photometric precision, constraining observations to ecliptic fields for 80-day campaigns.

title: Copper Tube Pinch Failure
abstract_text: While pinching copper tubes is a standard practice for many applications on Earth, it is not ideal for critical spaceflight applications due to uncertainty in the verification process and the potential for a seemingly “good” pinch to separate at a later time if a cold weld isn't achieved. However, if a copper tube must be pinched, it is useful to perform a Computerized Tomography (CT) scan and inverted metallography on the pinch if possible. The likelihood of damaging the pinch point also significantly decreases when a physical protective barrier (often an epoxy) is used to protect the pinch point.
driving_event_text: A copper tube was pinched as a test for the Mass Spectrometer observing lunar operations (MSolo) Calibration Gas System (CGS). A CT scan revealed that a pinch that was seemingly acceptable later separated to form a leak path.
lesson_text: The pinch was initially visually inspected and helium leak checked and no visual anomalies or leaks were detected. After a CT scan revealed that the seemingly acceptable pinch separated after the helium leak check to form a leak path, an investigation was started to try to form an empirical method for verifying that a pinch has cold welded correctly. The team first turned to industry experts on copper tube pinching. While they offered a plethora of tips and tricks to try to get the best pinch possible, they were not able to provide an empirical method to ensure that a flight pinch is sealed correctly. The MSolo team then performed CT scans and inverted metallography on several copper tube pinches in an attempt to find measurements on the pinch cross-section that could be taken to distinguish a “good” pinch from a “bad” one. The results were documented, but they varied and were not useful for definitively deeming a pinch to be acceptable or not. The CT scans and inverted metallography did, however, provide insight into the shape of the pinch. A pinch with a nominal shape is more likely to have cold welded correctly. This was useful in conjunction with how the operator “felt” the pinch went. The pinch tool operator pinched several dozen practice samples and could tell from experience when a pinch didn't feel right. When some of those samples were CT scanned, the scan showed an off-nominal shape. An off-nominal shape does not guarantee that the pinch did not cold weld but when conducting our final flight pinch, we re-pinched to increase the chances of flying a successful pinch with a nominal shape. The team also took into account some lessons learned from copper tube pinch failures for spaceflight applications in the past. The pinch point is delicate and can potentially leak if impacted. These lessons learned urged those using pinched copper tubes in spaceflight applications to protect the pinch point of the copper tube during transit and in-flight, if possible.
recommendation_text: Have pinch tool operator perform several practice pinches until he/she has an intuitive feel for the pinch tool kickback that occurs for a quotgoodquot pinch versus a quotbadquot pinch. Perform CT scans and/or inverted metallography to observe the shape of the pinch. Use some means of protection for the pinch point during transit and in-flight, if possible.

evidence.quote: The pinch was initially visually inspected and helium leak checked and no visual anomalies or leaks were detected. After a CT scan revealed that the seemingly acceptable pinch separated after the helium leak check to form a leak path.

pinching copper tubes is a standard practice for many applications on Earth, it is not ideal for critical spaceflight applications due to uncertainty in the verification process and the potential for a seemingly 'good' pinch to separate at

The pinch tool operator pinched several dozen practice samples and could tell from experience when a pinch didn't feel right...An off-nominal shape does not guarantee that the pinch did not cold weld but when conducting our final flight pin

impact_notes: Copper tube pinch failure created a leak path in MSolo Calibration Gas System, risking mission-critical gas containment. Standard Earth practices proved inadequate for spaceflight; required development of empirical verification methods and protective measures.

title: Cable Harness Wiring and Connector Anomalies Caused by Induced Damage in Human Spaceflight Vehicles
program_relation: Space Shuttle Program, Commercial Crewed Spacecraft
abstract_text: Early indications show that the commercial spacecraft developers and operators are experiencing a reduced incidence of wiring anomalies compared to the Space Shuttle Program (SSP). There are differences in implementation of wiring designs between the new vehicles and the Space Shuttle. Recognition of these differences and an appreciation of where SSP failure mechanisms can pose a risk to new crewed launch vehicles and spacecraft can help to manage the incidence of wiring anomalies. Decisions that reduce wire inspection and testing post installation may need to be revisited if the factors resulting in reduced wire incidents change. ​​​​​​​
driving_event_text: NASA has found that the commercial spacecraft developers and operators have a reduced incidence of wiring anomalies compared to the Space Shuttle and has identified causes for this reduced incidence. This reduction can potentially be attributed to: Limited re-use compared to the SSP Reduced exposed wiring in modern spacecraft routing designs New vehicles being integrated in a single orientation, allowing better access control, and enable ground crew to avoid sensitive areas and causing collateral damage The reduced size of ground crew and scope of processing Less wire, smaller length of wire New insulations that replaced Kapton Use of Scoop Proof MIL-DTL-38999 connectors to prevent damaging pins during connector mating System Separation System Architecture (avionics box consolidation and greater incorporation of printed circuit boards (PCB) to eliminate wiring Lower Level of Inspections and Reporting/Inspection Criteria Reduced testing scope for the detection of post-installation failures Additionally, as NASA moves away from legacy quality standards to industry consensus standards and commercial providers transition to a re-use production environment with larger systems and ground crews, there is a greater risk of increase incidence of wire and harness anomalies pre-launch. A consequence of the move to industry standards is the loss of user post-installation testing with reliance on manufacturing testing alone. NASA can offer relevant wire anomaly experience to help manage the incidence of wiring anomalies.
lesson_text: As a result of many years of expensive lessons learned, NASA imposed electrical tests after installation in locations where it was determined that the majority of cable / wiring harness damage occurs during installation and maintenance actions. The probability of incurring immediate or latent damage depends on the robustness of the cable / harness assembly and the installation itself. Installation of a cable / harness assembly into an avionics box would not be expected to carry as much risk as installation / routing of the cable / harness assembly through the bulkheads and wiring trays of a large vehicle. NASA has discovered wiring damage issues in every crewed vehicle system (Apollo, Shuttle, ISS, and now Orion) as well as some issues in commercial spacecraft developers and operators’ vehicles. While it is agreed that post-installation electrical testing takes time (and by extension increased cost), it is often less difficult (and less costly) to find and repair damage BEFORE vehicle integration proceeds to the point that access is no longer possible without disassembly of the vehicle. Functional testing may not identify a degraded signal path or latent defect condition. Applying a high voltage does have a high probability of locating a degraded dielectric path or latent defect. Of the three (3) tests listed in NASA-STD-8739.4, Rev A, Workmanship Standard for Crimping, Interconnecting Cables, Harnesses, and Wiring, [18.2.8], the two (2) most commonly used are (in sequence): (1) Continuity and Shorts, and (2) Insulation Resistance (IR). These electrical tests will not identify a potential short circuit that may occur during the dynamic loading phase of a flight if there is no detectable amount of current leakage during test – or if the test wasn’t required because the wiring had been previously tested. The most notorious example is the loss of two main engine control units during launch of STS-93 due to a wire in the payload bay rubbing against the sharp edge of a screw head and shorting as it moved under launch load. This was a close call. Specific lessons learned on post installation testing are: All boxes, mounted or not, should be isolated from the potential harmful effects of the test voltages. Ensure avionics boxes will not be affected by high voltage testing by not connecting harnesses to mounted boxes. Verify the system complies with NASA Standard 8739.4 for assembly and integration: NASA-STD-8739.4, unless tailored, requires the integrators to perform dielectric withstanding voltage (DWV) tests on all harnesses after installation. This requirement is imposed to identify potential damage caused during the installation of the harnesses and provides guidance on applying IR and DWV to wire harnesses. Application of post installation testing Is especially important in difficult-to-access areas where physical access to perform visual inspection is hampered, high traffic areas, and where harnesses are manipulated to install them. Access to the harness connectors and to the harnesses themselves drives the integration approach to provide field joints to simplify testing, eliminate very long test harnesses, and excessive manipulation of the harnesses to access them safely. If excessive manipulations of the harnesses are required, then the benefit of the post installation testing is negated due to the additional handling and potential to damage the harnesses during testing. NASA’s has not yet seen the same incidence of discrepancies in the new generation of vehicles as was seen in the historical data of discrepancies experience on Apollo and Space Shuttle. The Space Shuttle has a complex wiring system with approximately 600 harnesses, 7,000 connectors, 120,000 wire segm ents and 228 miles of wire. NASA’s experience shows handling-induced damage or damage resulting from work in close proximity is a primary source of wiring anomalies. Figure 1 shows a breakdown of the types of wire related problems encountered in the Orbiter fleet. The types of wire related problems shown include chafe protection, Kapton damage, jacket damage, shield damage, exposed conductor, and conductor damage. Figure 1. Orbiter Wiring Problem Breakdown. Adapted from: Evaluation of Cable Harness Post-Installation Testing: Part B, NASA/TP-2011-216300R, p.10, https://ntrs.nasa.gov/citations/20110014609 . A detailed examination of the number of problem reports on Orbiter wiring per year from 1979 to 2007 shows a relationship between the number of Wire Problem Reports (PRs) per year (bars) and flights per year. From the data, shown in Figure 2, one key point is that there is no indication that wiring PRs decrease with vehicle maturity in the case of the Orbiter. Also, per Orbiter Electrical PK-F Electrical Division, Kennedy Space Center), there are two major reasons that Orbiter wiring PRs increase during low flight rate years. First, inspections of wiring go up during those years. Second, as the flight rate decreases there is more traffic in the ship resulting in an increase in collateral damage. Figure 2. Shuttle (Orbiter) Wiring Problem Reports and Flight Rate from 1979-2000 (Note that the flight rate data is erroneously shifted to the right one year.) Adapted from: Evaluation of Cable Harness Post-Installation Testing: Part B, NASA/TP-2011-216300R, p.9, https://ntrs.nasa.gov/citations/20110014609 . ​​​​​ NASA did conduct a lessons-learned review after the Space Shuttle was retired and the following observation were made with respect to the electrical wiring system: It is important to perform detailed closeout inspection after work on cables or connectors. Wiring is easily damaged by nearby work activity. Training of personnel is critical both to prevent damage and to detect damage. Wire chafe protection should be used to protect critical* wiring. Separate critical* redundant wire runs and/or install barrier materials to protect against the effects of arc tracking. The effects of aging, operation, environmental exposure, re-use should be factored into the expected operational life of the wiring system * Critical wires are wires that, should they fail, pose a catastrophic hazard (loss of crew risk or loss of mission) risk Finally, it should be noted that several high-profile failures in NASA history have included wiring faults and handling-induced damage as related causes. Included in these are the Apollo 1 and Apollo 13 incidents. The Findings for the Apollo 1 fire, which resulted in the death of the crew, was that it is most probable that the fire was initiated by an electric arc and was most probably brought about by some minor malfunction or failure of equipment or wire insulation. The components involved had a history of many removals and of technical difficulties with design and installation features making removal or repair difficult. In addition, deficiencies in design, manufacture, installation, rework and quality control existed in the Teflon electrical wiring. The report conclusion included a recommendation that rigorous inspection at all stages of wiring design, manufacture, and installation be enforced. Similarly, on Apollo 13, the No. 2 oxygen tank, had been previously installed in the service module of Apollo 10, but was removed for modification (and was damaged in the process of removal). At 55 hours 55 minutes, oxygen tank No. 2 catastrophically failed, causing No. 1 tank also to fail. The Apollo 13 command module’s normal supply of electricity, light, and water was lost when they were about 200,000 miles from Earth. Subsequent investigation of the accident determined the cause of the combustion was most probably the ignition of Teflon wire insulation on the fan motors wires, caused by electric arcs in this wiring. In both cases, handling-induced damage as well as wire insulation failure were contributors. The current commercial spacecraft developers and operators’ space vehicles appear to benefit from more simplified designs and lower technician traffic during integration. In the case of the larger NASA vehicles, ground processing necessarily involved non-wire-oriented staff whose integration work did not appreciate the susceptibilities for wire damage.
recommendation_text: As commercial vehicles and other NASA vehicles (e.g. Orion SLS) that return to earth and undergo maintenance proceed into the production and re-use of human-rated vehicles, and as the wiring system grows in these designs, shared lessons learned from NASA regarding wire anomalies can aid in the prevention of failures. This includes awareness of human factors such as ease of access, harness routing for better installation/maintenance access, and proximity to obstructions. Additionally, employ harness design practices that minimize single point failures and conduct IR and DWV tests on installed harnesses. ​​​​​​ References NASA-STD-8739.4, Rev A, Workmanship Standard for Crimping, Interconnecting Cables, Harnesses, and Wiring, 2016. Evaluation of Cable Harness Post-Installation Testing: Part B, NASA/TP-2011-216300R, https://ntrs.nasa.gov/citations/20110014609 .

evidence.quote: NASA imposed electrical tests after installation in locations where it was determined that the majority of cable / wiring harness damage occurs during installation and maintenance actions.
A consequence of the move to industry standards is the loss of user post-installation testing with reliance on manufacturing testing alone.
ground processing necessarily involved non-wire-oriented staff whose integration work did not appreciate the susceptibilities for wire damage.

impact_notes: Apollo 1 fire resulted in crew fatality; Apollo 13 oxygen tank failure caused loss of electrical power, water, and light 200,000 miles from Earth. Current risk of increased wiring anomalies in production re-use vehicles if post-installation testing and inspection cadence are reduced.

title: SharePoint Nintex Licensing
abstract_text: KSC SharePoint 2019: The Nintex Workflow/Forms license expired, causing no workflows to run during the downtime to get new licenses.
driving_event_text: KSC recently upgraded from SharePoint 2016 (a web-based collaboration platform) to SharePoint 2019. A third party tool, Nintex Workflow used to automate business processes in SharePoint, only allowed for a 30 day migration license. All SharePoint sites were migrated in time. However, the migration license was not updated to the production license . When the migration license ran out, users were not able to use Nintex workflow or forms.
lesson_text: We learned that we need to improve our communication for license upgrades and plan the steps involved. It took almost 6 hours for the new licenses to be acquired.
recommendation_text: Corrective Action Plan: Improve our communication with vendors to ensure we have a thorough understanding of the license upgrade steps. Add a lessons learned to the SharePoint 2019 Upgrade Project and to the Knowledge Management Articles webpage for future reference. Scheduled a quarterly review of SharePoint licensing so the team and the NASA point of contact are aware of upcoming expirations.

evidence.quote: the migration license was not updated to the production license. When the migration license ran out, users were not able to use Nintex workflow or forms.
We learned that we need to improve our communication for license upgrades and plan the steps involved. It took almost 6 hours for the new licenses to be acquired.
Scheduled a quarterly review of SharePoint licensing so the team and the NASA point of contact are aware of upcoming expirations.

impact_notes: 6-hour downtime for all Nintex workflow and forms functionality across SharePoint 2019 platform; operational impact to business process automation at KSC.

title: E-1 Triethyl Aluminum-Triethyl Borane (TEA-TEB) System Contamination
abstract_text: On June 28th, 2022, during post test inspections, test operations personnel noted that the E-1 Cell 2 Triethyl Aluminum-Triethyl Borane (TEA-TEB) injection supply tubing was warm to the touch. This indicated that there could have been a TEA-TEB reaction occurring inside the tubing. Personnel identified the source of air was coming from FireX Master Facility Panel (MFP) Station 6. No personal injury or facility damage was sustained. Background The FireX MFP Station 6 was originally designed and installed to service valve actuation of all stand FireX systems, and as such the system is branched and routed throughout the stand and all three E-1 test cell locations. TEA-TEB is a pyrophoric liquid which ignites when exposed to air and water, and is often used as an ignition method for rocket testing. It is stable when stored and operated with dry and inert purges until ready to be injected into the rocket components for ignition. The TEA-TEB storage system and run system had been installed for a previous test project in Cell 1 in 2016. The design tapped into a nearby GN system downstream from MFP Station 6 for the required GN purge and pressurization. At this time, MFP drawing and panel labeling was not changed to reflect the additional purpose of Station 6 for TEA-TEB purging. Configuration Control of Inactive Systems In preparation for inclement weather in September 2021, the FireX MFP supply was swapped from gaseous nitrogen to high pressure air (HPA) to conserve nitrogen usage at E-1 and had remained in this configuration ever since. The TEA-TEB system was inactive at the time of the configuration change as the Cell 1 project had concluded and the upcoming Cell 2 project was still in the design/build phase. For this new project, a new TEA-TEB run system was added to Cell 2 in 2022 and was designed to utilize the same TEA-TEB storage system and GN source as the Cell 1 system.
driving_event_text: Root Cause In preparation for inclement weather in September 2021, the FireX MFP supply was swapped from gaseous nitrogen to high pressure air to conserve nitrogen usage at E1. The FireX MFP remained in HPA service post hurricane season to continue to conserve nitrogen usage. E-1 FireX Master Facility Panel Station 6 is used to purge the TEA-TEB. By using this purge/press system with the HPA supply, air was introduced into the TEA-TEB system. Contributing Factors: The Station 6 description did not state that Station 6 was serving as a purge to the TEA-TEB System. Test Operations made a time-critical decision to respond to a stressful situation due to limited deliveries of Liquid Nitrogen to the High-Pressure Gas Facility post Hurricane Ida. The implementation proceeded as a permanent change instead of a temporary change. This did not allow the Test Operations Team a chance to fully review the system configuration without the stress of limited GN and post major hurricane.
lesson_text: Corrective Actions and Recommendations The air source was removed, and the MFP drawing was labeled for “GN service only” and to indicate that Station 6 supplies the TEA-TEB Cart Pressurization and Purge. These labels were also physically added to the MFP Station 6 panel for operators in the field. In addition, a label was added to the TEA-TEB cart door indicating that GN source pressure was routed from MFP Station 6. Ideally, the team would like to fabricate and install a separate and dedicated GN-only station exclusively for the TEA-TEB system. Some other long term actions are being considered for further action. Infrared cameras could be utilized to detect thermal heating in TEA-TEB system tubing, enabling the test conductor to monitor system conditions prior to releasing personnel back to the test stand facility. This capability is already used in other areas, particularly on hydrogen systems. The team is also exploring ways to mistake proof the design and configuration control processes, to use automated systems to identify and flag systems which have commodity restrictions. SEE ATTACHED DOCUMENT
recommendation_text: Ensure proper labeling of system source panels, and especially pay attention to restrictions of commodity sources to avoid unintentional contamination. Always use processes which establish constraints or required verifications prior to reactivating systems which have been inactive for a period of time.

evidence.quote: In preparation for inclement weather in September 2021, the FireX MFP supply was swapped from gaseous nitrogen to high pressure air...The FireX MFP remained in HPA service post hurricane season...By using this purge/press system with the HP

The TEA-TEB system was inactive at the time of the configuration change...At this time, MFP drawing and panel labeling was not changed to reflect the additional purpose of Station 6 for TEA-TEB purging.

The implementation proceeded as a permanent change instead of a temporary change. This did not allow the Test Operations Team a chance to fully review the system configuration without the stress of limited GN and post major hurricane.

impact_notes: Pyrophoric TEA-TEB contamination with air created ignition hazard during test operations. No injury or facility damage occurred, but incident exposed critical safety gap in configuration control of inactive systems with commodity restrictions.

title: Improved Operations and Backlog Management of NANADARTS at GRC
abstract_text: NASA Advisories, Notices and Alerts Distribution and Response Tracking System (NANADARTS) is the system that makes Government-Industry Data Exchange Program (GIDEP) Notices, NASA Advisories, and other similar documents available to the entire NASA workforce and also serves as the agency's official tool for closed loop reporting of these documents per NPR 8735.1. The incident under discussion for this “lessons learned” is the accumulation of a massive backlog of NANADARTS documents which was comprised of documents that went undistributed by center coordinators and documents that went unevaluated by required responders. This accumulation took place over a three-year period, and peaked in the year 2021, with a total backlog of several thousand documents.
driving_event_text: Per NPR 8735.1, NASA programs and projects must implement closed-loop reporting via NANADARTS whenever a GIDEP notice or NASA advisory may impact “critical work” as defined in NPD 8730.5. Therefore, all programs or projects that perform critical work must participate. NASA has an agency GIDEP/NANADARTS coordinator, and each center has two center GIDEP/NANADARTS coordinators. The agency GIDEP/NANADARTS coordinator and their team are responsible for extracting any new NASA-relevant GIDEP notices from GIDEP, and along with any newly released NASA Advisories, distributing these via the NANADARTS website to each of the center GIDEP/NANADARTS coordinators. The center coordinators are responsible for distributing these, again via NANADARTS, to the participating programs and projects. These programs and projects will have a dedicated required responder who is responsible for receiving and evaluating these GIDEP notices and NASA advisories via NANADARTS. These documents are evaluated for usage by the required responders within the NANADARTS system and this information is then reported out to the agency. Unfortunately, due to the causes described below, and not necessarily the fault of any one person, unsent and unanswered documents began accumulating both on the coordinators’ end, and the required responders’ end, which ultimately led to a dangerously large backlog. Distribution of documents from the agency coordinator to the center coordinators is irregular. Sometimes a hundred new documents may be sent from the agency coordinator to the center coordinators in a day. Sometimes weeks will go by with zero new documents. This is not necessarily the fault of anyone. This is determined by external factors – mainly the rate at which new GIDEP notices have been submitted to GIDEP by other agencies and companies, and how many of those are relevant to NASA. This has the effect of making it difficult both for center coordinators and required responders to develop a regular routine. The center coordinators at GRC do not have any assigned time to perform NANADARTS duties, and there exists no WBS, which leads to assigned work taking precedence over NANADARTS. Center-wide there has been a history of inadequate awareness levels and somewhat low priority given to NANADARTS activities. For example, most GRC employees have never heard of NANADARTS and are unaware of how it relates to GIDEP, nor does there exist much familiarity with either the NPR or GRC work instruction that discusses it. Also, it was often not well-known across the center which projects needed to participate, how to participate, why it’s important, and what would be the potential consequences of not participating. Consequently, NANADARTS responsibilities in the past have often been given very low priority. This significant backlog was noted as a major finding in an external audit.
lesson_text: Several steps were taken to successfully eliminate the backlog: 1. As a result of the external audit finding, center coordinators, branch chiefs, and upper management decided on giving the NANADARTS process a higher priority and greater visibility by having center coordinators report status updates of backlog counts at the monthly Safety & Mission Assurance Management Board meetings. Management from across the center attends this meeting and was made aware of the seriousness of this issue through these out-briefs. This action continued until the backlog was eliminated. 2. To deal with the irregular pacing of incoming alerts and notices, to increase communication between everyone involved, and to promote a greater sense of importance to this task, center coordinators developed a more regular routine for the benefit of both themselves and the required responders. Center coordinators did this by utilizing NANADARTS user tools to compile backlog counts at the start of every month, and then emailing these numbers as monthly status updates to each required responder and their project manager. The required responders and Project Managers were also reminded that these backlog counts were being reported up the chain at the monthly S&MA Management Board meetings, as described above. 3. Center coordinators increased awareness of the NANADARTS process: a. Center coordinators gave presentations to individual codes on the NANADARTS process so that the Program/Project Managers could better understand the process, the importance of it, and to determine whether their program or project should be participating. b. Center coordinators were able to add a link to the NANADARTS website on GRC’s one-stop-shop front page, “Inside Glenn,” under the “Employee Tools” dropdown. (https://nasa.sharepoint.com/sites/grc) c. Center coordinators hosted a booth for GIDEP/NANADARTS activities at GRC’s annual Safety Day and will continue to do so at future safety days. 4. GRC center coordinators reached out to other center’s coordinators for guidance: a. Other center’s coordinators provided guidance on how they conducted NANADARTS training to required responders and project managers. The GRC center coordinators incorporated some of the other centers’ training documentation into their own to improve their training process. Once GRC’s training materials were updated, training sessions were held with all required responders and their project managers. b. Other center’s coordinators also provided their centers’ work instructions on NANADARTS. The GRC center coordinators took guidance from these documents and published a new revision of the center’s work instruction offering finer details and more direction. 5. Center coordinators invited all other NASA center coordinators to attend a face-to-face event at GRC to discuss other ways to improve how NANADARTS is implemented both center-wide and agency-wide.
recommendation_text: There should be center-wide awareness of NANADARTS. Programs and projects should be aware of whether they should be participating, how they participate, why it’s important, and what the potential consequences are of not participating. NANADARTS documents should be distributed by the center coordinators on a regular basis to the appropriate required responders. Then, these documents should be responded to in a timely manner by those required responders Additional steps that could be taken: The center coordinators at GRC do not have any assigned time to perform NANADARTS duties, and there exists no WBS, which leads to assigned work taking precedence over NANADARTS. Additionally, coordinators are having to charge time spent on NANADARTS activities to their projects. Most other NASA centers have funding for their center coordinators, while GRC does not.

evidence.quote: The center coordinators at GRC do not have any assigned time to perform NANADARTS duties, and there exists no WBS, which leads to assigned work taking precedence over NANADARTS.
unsent and unanswered documents began accumulating both on the coordinators' end, and the required responders' end, which ultimately led to a dangerously large backlog
making it difficult both for center coordinators and required responders to develop a regular routine

impact_notes: Accumulation of several thousand undistributed and unevaluated critical safety documents over three years created dangerously large backlog, potentially delaying critical work impact assessments and closed-loop reporting compliance per NPR 8735.1.

title: Displacement of E-1 High Pressure Liquid Hydrogen Vent Line to Flare Stack
nasa_topics: Mission Operations and Ground Support Systems
abstract_text: On the morning of Monday September 25, 2023, a drive-by inspection of the E1 liquid hydrogen (LH) flare stack was performed before typical test activities revealing a detached rain shield on the vent line. Upon further inspection that morning, the vent piping was observed to be displaced north up to 36 inches, had buckling damage on the 42” Expandable Joint (EJ) assembly, damage to multiple supports, and impact damage of the 30” elbow just downstream of the 30” EJ assembly.
driving_event_text: The damage to the vent line is believed to have occurred during Thrust Chamber Assembly ( TCA) testing on Thursday, September 21, during the first test of the day. The fuel systems being used for TCA testing are connected to the Low Pressure (LP) and High Pressure (HP) vent systems and are being used in Liquid Natural Gas (LNG) service. The Cell 3 interface bleed valve that discharges to the HP LH Vent system was inadvertently left at 100% open during the chill-in process for longer than the typical amount of time. Since this valve is usually at a lower set point or intermittently closed during the chill-in process, more than the typical amount of LNG flowed into the vent line as pre-test procedures continued as normal.
lesson_text: The root causes of this Undesired Outcome (UO) are identified as follows: the project design processes did not identify the vent system for additional evaluation, the Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) did not identify the hazard, and there was complacency related to the vent line. The design process looked at the adequacy of the Run Tank and connected run line piping, and the HP Flare Stack for LNG service, but it did not evaluate the vent piping between the run system and the Flare Stack. During the safety review of the design, which has multiple aspects, the hazard related to liquid and the increased loads was not identified. Finally, the complacency related to the vent line developed over time. The vent line was not maintained or inspected between tests or on a regular basis. Also, the system identity includes LH, so liquid in the system was not seen as an issue. Anecdotally, LH has been seen discharging from the flare stack, so system integrity was assumed to be adequate for current test operations. Contributing factors for the failure are related to the inadequacy of the HP LH Vent System for LNG. Originally, the vent system was design for hydrogen service, where the worst-case loads were from venting of the HP LH Run tank at maximum pressure. LNG has a density much greater than gaseous hydrogen, so produces much higher momentum loads on the support structure when moved at similar velocities. More capable support structure would have helped but likely not prevented the UO. Lastly, had the time between the vent line being overflown with LNG and test been longer, the vent line would have warmed up enough to vaporize all the liquid in the northern section that was damaged. Additionally, the HP vent line was overflown with LNG during chill-in because the operator left the bleed valve 100% open for longer than intended. This action was not constrained, and at the time, not seen as an issue. If liquid had been identified as an issue, the Detailed Operating Procedures ( DOP) would have likely identified constraints for chill-in procedure. Moreover, if the Cell 3 bleed had not been connected to the HP LH Vent System, this UO would have been prevented. Using the HP Vent system for disposal of ambient cryogenic propellants, even liquid hydrogen, is ill-advised.
recommendation_text: Disciplined configuration control practices are vital to ensure that facility systems are built and maintained to the specified design. It is important to consider all the differences between the commodity used and how it will affect the entire system. Once these effects are known, they need to be clearly understood by operators and documented. Operators and technicians need to understand the limits of the system for when they need to adjust procedure for mission success. This can be difficult when encountering a failure of imagination. Additionally, when changing configuration, all individual systems must be analyzed even when a system is known to be robust.

evidence.quote: the project design processes did not identify the vent system for additional evaluation, the Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) did not identify the hazard
LNG has a density much greater than gaseous hydrogen, so produces much higher momentum loads on the support structure when moved at similar velocities
The vent line was not maintained or inspected between tests or on a regular basis. Also, the system identity includes LH, so liquid in the system was not seen as an issue

impact_notes: Vent line displaced 36 inches with buckling damage to 42" expandable joint, damage to multiple supports, and impact damage to 30" elbow. Incident occurred during test operations; no stated injuries but represents significant facility damage and operational disruption.

title: Launch Equipment Test Facility (LETF) Vehicle Motion Simulator (VMS) Hydraulic System Failure Resulted in $500K in Electrical System Damage.
nasa_topics: Engineering design and project processes and standards
abstract_text: On July 27, 2023, an incident occurred at the Kennedy Space Center (KSC) Launch Equipment Test Facility (LETF) Vehicle Motion Simulator (VMS). While pressurizing the LETF hydraulic pumps to supply hydraulic fluid to VMS-2 (to reposition it for Exploration Upper Stage Umbilical preparations), a NOSHOK pressure gauge in the LETF Pump House ruptured at 9:48 AM. The gauge failed at the normal operating pressure of 3,000 pounds per square inch gauge, resulting in approximately 75 gallons of hydraulic fluid (Mobil Dynamo, Turbine, Engine (DTE)-25) spraying throughout the Pump House. No personnel were injured. However, the incident resulted in approximately $500,000 in replacement costs for damaged electrical equipment. The mission or launch schedule was not impacted. The Mishap Investigation Team (MIT) determined that the damage resulted from the combination of: A failed hydraulic analog pressure gauge, caused by inadequate solder adhesion between the gauge bourdon tube and socket. High-value electronic equipment enclosures near the oil spray, which had top-entered conduit feeder assembly fittings that were not adequately sealed or protected, allowing oil to enter the electrical enclosures.
driving_event_text: During hydraulic system pressurization for LETF operations, a NOSHOK pressure gauge ruptured while operating at normal pressures. This resulted in approximately 75 gallons of hydraulic fluid being sprayed throughout the pump house. The oil intrusion damaged various pieces of electrical equipment, costing approximately $500,000 in damages. A full mishap investigation was conducted, and the resulting report was documented in the NASA Mishap Information System (NMIS) under tracking number #23-100895.
lesson_text: NASA Advisory Notice NA-KSC-2024-002 was created and released to notify other NASA Centers of the NOSHOK gauge failure. The notice was released through NANADARTS. The colocation of high dollar value electrical equipment and the high-pressure hydraulic system pumps revealed that KSC did not have requirements to assess worst-case consequences from fluid leaks. KSC updated design requirements documentation to assess fluid systems designs for the potential to damage adjacent high value equipment (HVE) due to worst-case catastrophic fluid system ruptures or leaks. Employees could not see inside pump house during operation and therfore did not easily discover the leak to shut the system down. Cameras were installed inside of the pump house to allow facility employees real-time visual access to the conditions inside of the pump house. Installation of a shunt trip was also performed to enable a quick shutdown of the system remotely if an off- nominal condition is detected. Other same model gauges on the system were inspected and found to be in a degareded condition. Gauges were found with corrosion to their inside housing, complete loss of gauge glycerin, a bent pointer, and missing or embedded plugs. Updated pressure system preventative maintenance documented procedures and training to include looking for common signs of wear and damage on pressure gauges.
recommendation_text: 1. Review NASA Advisory Notice NA-KSC-2024-002 and take action if needed. 2. Apply lessons learned in this mishap event to like systems and facilities that may be present at your Center/site.

evidence.quote: A failed hydraulic analog pressure gauge, caused by inadequate solder adhesion between the gauge bourdon tube and socket.

KSC did not have requirements to assess worst-case consequences from fluid leaks. KSC updated design requirements documentation to assess fluid systems designs for the potential to damage adjacent high value equipment.

Employees could not see inside pump house during operation and therefore did not easily discover the leak to shut the system down. Cameras were installed inside of the pump house to allow facility employees real-time visual access.

impact_notes: Approximately $500,000 in replacement costs for damaged electrical equipment. No personnel injuries. No mission or launch schedule impact.

title: Mate/Demate, Verify, and Document Connectors One-at-a-Time (2003)
abstract_text: An integration and test failure was traced to an unmated flight connector that was registered as mated in the mate/demate log. The root cause was attributed to the failure of a technician/engineer to pause after each mate/ demate operation to enter it in the log, and to obtain QA verification. Flight connectors located in crowded or constrained areas (as well as those in more visible areas) must be mated or demated one-at-a-time under QA supervision, and accurate documentation of the connector status must be maintained.
driving_event_text: When the navigation camera (Navcam) power was switched on during integration and test (I&T) of the Mars Exploration Rover (MER) spacecraft, voltage measurements showed no power or data signals within the Navcam circuits. The Navcam had worked properly when tested prior to an earlier removal and re-installation of the Rover Power Distribution Unit (RPDU). An investigation revealed an un-mated connector on the Nonvolatile Memory and Camera Interface Board (NVMCAM) within the Rover Electronics Module (REM) (Figure 1). The assembly procedure called for the mating of this connector, and the mate/demate log indicated that the connector had been mated. The root cause of the workmanship error was attributed to the failure of a technician/ engineer to pause after each mate/demate operation to enter it in the log, and to obtain Quality Assurance (QA) verification. A high connector density and an obstructed view within the REM make it difficult to verify the status of a single connector once multiple connectors have been mated. MER also has other high-density connection areas such as the Rover bulkheads (Figure 2). Although the impact of this error was minor, de-integration and rework to re-mate a connector in a highly integrated system like MER can pose a major risk to flight hardware. After this incident, the Assembly, Test, and Launch Operations (ATLO) manager mandated a “one at a time” practice of QA verification after every MER connector mate/demate, and it did not permit mate/demate of multiple connectors prior to obtaining QA support. References: JPL Problem/Failure Report No. Z79107, January 20, 2003. Crimping, Interconnecting Cables, Harnesses, and Wiring, NASA-STD-8739.4, February 9, 1998. Magellan Battery Fire, LLIS No. 0386, NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS), February 23, 1995. Mismate of Identical Connectors, ASTROS Project, 1988, LLIS No.0431, NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS), September 14, 1995. Electrical Connectors, LLIS No.0444, NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS), July 30, 1993. Electrical Connectors - Scout Program, LLIS No.0470, NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS), July 22, 1994. Additional Key Words: connector mating, connector demating, QA procedure, blind mate
lesson_text: Because the correct mating and demating of flight connectors is critical in producing a safe and fully functional component, it is imperative that detailed procedures be followed and that verification and documentation occurs at the appropriate points in time. Multiple connectors located in crowded or constrained areas (as well as those in more visible areas) should be mated one at a time and under the surveillance of Quality Assurance. Each mate or demate should be verified and appropriately documented so that the correct status of the hardware is maintained at all times.
recommendation_text: Revise mate/demate requirements to ensure that flight connectors located in crowded or constrained areas (as well as those in more visible areas) are mated or demated one at a time and under the supervision of QA. Each mate or demate in these types of areas should be verified by QA and documented, one at a time, to ensure that the status of the component is maintained at all times. In extreme visibility problem areas, the technician mating/demating activities and QA oversight shall be simultaneous.

evidence.quote: failure of a technician/engineer to pause after each mate/demate operation to enter it in the log, and to obtain QA verification
An unmated flight connector that was registered as mated in the mate/demate log
A high connector density and an obstructed view within the REM make it difficult to verify the status of a single connector once multiple connectors have been mated

impact_notes: Unmated connector caused loss of Navcam power/data signals during I&T; required de-integration and rework. Stated impact was minor, but potential for major risk to flight hardware in highly integrated system.
